title: $19bn in property deals done in just six months
full_text:
A series of big-ticket industrial and retail deals lifted the value of Australian commercial property transactions to $19bn in the first half of 2026 with more deals expected to close in the coming months.
The market overcame rising interest rates and geopolitical instability as institutions stepped up to expand upon the once favoured “beds and sheds” theme to also encompass the traditional field of shops.
Activity in the first half of 2026 was up 16 per cent on this time in 2025, according to preliminary CBRE data tallies of office, retail, industrial and logistics, hotel and living sector deals.
CBRE Pacific head of capital markets Flint Davidson noted that the first half numbers reflected a period of significant volatility in market conditions.
“Following strong momentum and elevated transaction activity in the new year, we entered a period of disruption driven by interest rates, inflation, and conflict in the Middle East before a level of stabilisation more recently,” Mr Davidson said.
“Against that backdrop, the fact that volumes still increased by 16 per cent year-on-year points to a market that is becoming more resilient to increasingly common shocks. Looking ahead, we expect a substantial pipeline of property assets to be brought to market in the second half.”
Retail was the most traded sector in the half, with $6.1bn in assets changing hands – up 4 per cent year-on-year. This included Lendlease APPF Retail’s $1.2bn sale to GPT’s funds unit of half stakes in Sunshine Plaza at Maroochydore in Queensland and Macarthur Square at Campbelltown in Sydney’s southwest.
Industrial and logistics ranked second with $5.5bn in trades, buoyed by Goodman Group’s $2.65bn deal with Washington H.Soul Pattinson and Company to take control of a series of large warehouse developments. Transaction activity was up 5 per cent year-on-year.
With offices coming back into focus, office sales were up 15 per cent year-on-year to $4.1bn – although this involved mostly Sydney deals, including the sale of the NBN headquarters. More transactions are expected to close in this half as privates and fund managers ink transactions they have had in train.
Meanwhile, activity in the living and hotel sectors rose significantly, albeit off last year’s low base, with $2bn in living deals and $1.2bn in hotel trades.
The first half numbers also highlight a rise in activity from domestic purchasers, with the level of offshore buying dropping 8 per cent year-on-year to $4bn – accounting for just 21 per cent of the total sales volume. Some offshore buyers are effectively steering clear of Victoria, which has allowed local buyers to step up.
CBRE head of capital markets research Tom Broderick said that domestic institutions and fund managers were getting more active while investment from offshore groups had slowed.
“Volatility at the global level often causes investors to pause and concentrate on their home markets, which is what we are seeing so far this year,” he said. The US was the top source market over the past 12 months, accounting for $2.7bn in transactions, followed by Japan ($1.5bn) and Singapore ($1.5bn).
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
